coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots for optical detection of fluoride ion in aqueous media
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Safavi, Afsaneh
Mohammadpour, Zahra
Zolghadr, Amin Reza
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a nanoprobe for sensing fluoride ion in aqueous solution has been introduced. The operation of this system is based on the quenching of the fluorescence signal of carbon nanodots (CDs) with a simple organic receptor. The linear trend in fluorescence signal with respect to fluoride concentration proved that Stern-Volmer equation was adapted successfully to the system with a linear range of 0–26.7mM and LOD value of 1.1×10−4 M. This system showed to be nonresponsive toward weak basic anions except for fluoride. Using toothpaste as a real sample, the level of fluoride was successfully measured by this method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanodots
4-mercaptophenylboronic acid
Fluoride sensor
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection procedure of the fluorescent probe
Analysis of fluoride in toothpaste and tap water
Sensitivity and selectivity of the fluorescent nanoprobe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (arrows on the picture, shows dots which are not clear in the first look) and (B) FTIR spectrum of synthesized CDs.

UV spectral change of MPBA (40μM) in water with (A) addition of fluoride ion (variable pH), (B) addition of OH− and (C) addition of fluoride in buffer with constant pH (phosphate–acetate buffer (0.05M, pH 5.5)).

Change in the extent of CDs signal quenching with MPBA upon addition of fluoride. Fluorescence signal of (a) CDs, (b) CDs+MPBA, (c) CDs+MPBA+F− ([F−]=1.46mM, [MPBA]=126μM) Inset: Change in the fluorescence signal of CDs in pure water in the absence (circle) and presence (triangle) of MPBA, ([MPBA] is NOT the optimized value).

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (Dash-Dot-Dot), after quenching with MPBA (red line) and subsequent addition of fluoride ion (Black solid lines) in (A) water with variable pH and (B) in constant pH (5.5).

Fluorescence spectral change of MPBA (blue circle) and PBA (red triangles) upon addition of fluoride ion in water. [MPBA/PBA]=2mM and the initial pH was 5.5. (inset: initial quenching of CDs signal with PBA (red dash dot line) and with MPBA (long dash blue line)).
Dependence of varying temperature on quenching of the CDs fluorescence signal by MPBA/F− ([MPBA] =2mM in pH=5.5).
Stern–volmer plot of fluorescence intensity ratios (F0/F at 340nm) versus fluoride concentrations from 0 to 26.7mM. Measurements were performed in pH 5.5, [MPBA]=2mM, excitation wavelength=310nm.

The quenching effect of other anions coexisted with fluoride on CDs/MPBA system. All of the anions have the same concentrations as fluoride (9×10−4 M). The PL intensity was monitored at excitation/emission wavelengths of 310/340nm.
Results of the analysis of real samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon nanodots for optical detection of fluoride ion in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shiraz University, Shiraz, 7194684795, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Shiraz University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Razi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a nanoprobe for sensing fluoride ion in aqueous solution has been introduced. The operation of this system is based on the quenching of the fluorescence signal of carbon nanodots (CDs) with a simple organic receptor. The linear trend in fluorescence signal with respect to fluoride concentration proved that Stern-Volmer equation was adapted successfully to the system with a linear range of 0–26.7mM and LOD value of 1.1×10−4 M. This system showed to be nonresponsive toward weak basic anions except for fluoride. Using toothpaste as a real sample, the level of fluoride was successfully measured by this method.

ce:section.ce:para: Growing of the health risks associated with high levels of toxic anions like fluoride in drinking water, makes fluoride ion representing one of the most attractive sensing targets. While water fluoridation has beneficial effects to dental caries, excessive intake of this ion also gives rise to dental/skeletal fluorosis [1], osteoporosis [2] and inhibition of neurotransmitter biosynthesis in foetuses [3]. The maximum authorized value of fluoride in drinking water is 4mg/L, which is dictated by Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) as an invaluable reference source for management of drinking water quality [4].

To date, numerous research studies have directed their efforts towards designing organic receptors to interact with fluoride based on different strategies. The hydrogen bonding between the active NH [5] group of (thio) ureas [6], amides [7], pyrrole [8] and imidazolium has been accounted for fluoride sensing in different solvents. The acidic hydrogen in these moieties provides a tendency to form hydrogen bonds with fluoride. Although some of these probes operate selectively toward fluoride ion, the complicated structures of these synthetic molecules necessitating multiple synthetic steps as well as inapplicability of these molecules in aqueous solutions, make the two major concerns of using these probes practically [9]. The high affinity of fluoride to silicon is another chemical interaction responsible for fluoride sensing [10]. Park et al. [11] successfully developed a water soluble fluorescent probe based on the cleavage of SiO bond by fluoride, and used that for cell imaging. Despite the good sensitivity and selectivity of this sensor, the duration time for detection of a single concentration extended to 4h; possibly make an entire analysis last more than 24h.

Another famous chemical interaction is the Lewis acid–base reaction based on the affinity of boron toward the fluoride anion. Gabbai et al. have an extensive research experience in designing fluoride sensors by taking into consideration the strength of the BF bond [12]. They investigated the fluoride binding capability of cationic boranes which are based on the coulombic host-guest interactions. The operation of these cationic molecular receptors [13] in methanol–water mixtures has been proven to be good in terms of sensitivity and selectivity. Some of these organic boranes however, operated under the biphasic conditions (CHCl3–H2O) [14]. In fact, restrictions on the use of organic solvents, due to environmental concerns, encouraged researchers to develop water soluble nanoprobes.

With the introduction of nanotechnology, the opportunity for exploring water soluble probes for fluoride sensing is now available to researchers [15]. All these nanoprobes exhibited water solubility, the most important factor for fluoride sensing in real samples. Xia et al. [16] demonstrated the versatility of Quantum dots (QDs) in anion sensing by choosing fluorescence modulating strategies. Xue et al. [17] reported a simple fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system of QDs and gold nanoparticles where the FRET efficiency was modulated based on the Lewis acid–base interaction of fluoride ion and boron atom. In 2013, a turn off nano probe (Zr (CDs–COO)2EDTA) was introduced by Liu et al. [15]. The stronger affinity of fluoride toward Zr(H2O)2EDTA compared to the carboxylic groups on the surface of carbon nanodots (CDs) caused quenching of the fluorescence signal. Although this strategy provided a sensitive and selective route for fluoride sensing, the sequential heating and cooling of the working solution for each fluoride concentration made the analysis time consuming.

In the present report, water soluble CDs have been used as the fluorophore. No functionalization was done on the surface of these nano materials. 4-mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA) acted as a simple organic receptor with quenching ability of the fluorescence signal. This molecule has two centers for fluoride reaction, the boronic acid as Lewis acidic moiety and SH for BrØnsted acid–base reaction. The covalent reaction of fluoride to boronic acid has been widely used in literature for sensing of fluoride in different solvents [18]. It has been reported that trifluoroboronate anion which is a tetrahedral fluoride adduct can quench the fluorescence signal of a fluorophore directly attached to this moiety via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism [19]. Since the surface of the synthesized CDs in this work is covered by oxygenated groups, we suppose that this side of the receptor can attach to the surface of CDs through hydrogen bonding and make the signal quenching. On the other hand, it has been proven that thiolate ligands can influence the photophysical properties of QDs through producing the hole traps on the surface and decrease the fluorescence signal [20]. In fact, the purpose of choosing MPBA was to increase signal quenching from both sides. Since fluoride ion is both basic anion and also has nucleophilic properties it was expected that this simple molecule can act as a dual channel quencher and make the signal quenching more efficient. However, as will be discussed later, only the BrØnsted acid base reaction between fluoride and SH group of the receptor accounted for signal quenching.

4-Mercaptophenylboronic acid (MPBA), phenylboronic acid (PBA), sodium chloride, sodium bromide, sodium iodide, sodium nitrate, sodium sulfate, sodium fluoride, 1-iodooctane, pyridine, diethyl ether were purchased from Merck.

A microwave oven (MW 350 GW/GS) was used for the synthesis of CDs. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Perkin Elmer fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS45) with a 1.0cm quartz cell. The absorption spectra were recorded by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a 1-cm path length quartz cuvette. TEM image was taken by a Zeiss-EM10C device at 80KV. FTIR spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu FTIR 8000 series.

Carbon nanodots were synthesized from N-octylpyridinum hexafluorophosphate (OPPF6) by a microwave assisted ionic liquid method [21]. Synthesis of OPPF6 was done according to the reported procedure [22]. Briefly, a desired amount of ionic liquid (IL) (about 50mg) was placed in a test tube and heated in microwave oven (Power, P =1000W) for approximately 2min. Changing the color of IL from colorless to dark brown implicated the production of CDs. Water soluble CDs were extracted via 4mL of distilled water and sonicated in an ultrasonic bath for 3min. Finally, the solution was filtered through a 0.2μm filter membrane and stored in 4°C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection procedure of the fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: 5μL of the prepared CDs was injected into 3mL aqueous solution. Then, MPBA/PBA (35mM, in ethanol) was added to the final optimized concentration of 2mM. The pH was adjusted to 5.5, and then different concentrations of fluoride ion were added. For each point, after 5min incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of fluoride in toothpaste and tap water
ce:section.ce:para: Toothpaste from Crest Company was chosen for analysis of the fluoride ion content. The amount of fluoride claimed on this mouthwash product was 0.321%. To prepare the sample, a simple procedure was carried out. 5.667g toothpaste was accurately weighed and refluxed in 30.0mL water at 70°C for 3h. The resulting white suspension was cooled to room temperature and then centrifuged several times, each for 30min at 3900rpm to remove solids. Excess Al(NO3)3 was added to the supernatant for precipitation of phosphate. The turbid solution was centrifuged and concentrated by evaporation of the solvent. A small aliquot of this concentrated solution was spiked into 3mL of CDs/MPBA mixture and the value of fluoride was calculated by standard addition method. The next real sample analyzed in this work was river water with no detectable fluoride in the concentration range of the present work. For river water preparation, 25mL of river water was diluted two fold. 3mL of this solution was placed in the cell. CDs and MPBA were added with pH adjusted to 5.5, then various fluoride concentrations were spiked.

Highly fluorescent CDs has been synthesized and well characterized in our research group [21]. An ionic liquid (IL), OPPF6 was used as the precursor. The average size of 4.3nm was supported by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as illustrated in Fig. 1A. The solution of CDs displayed three absorption bands centered at 315, 322 and 330nm. Fluorescence spectra of CDs with an excitation and emission wavelengths of 310 and 340nm, respectively, are presented in Fig. S1. Fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured as 6.7ns [21]. Water solubility of CDs in this study was indicative of their surface coverage by OH (3433cm−1), CO (1635cm−1) and COC (1296cm−1) as confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). There is a report of CDs synthesis which similarly used ILs as precursors and microwave as the synthetic method [23]. The calculated quantum yield is almost the same as what was obtained for our CDs (about 26–27%). However, the present synthetic procedure is much simpler because centrifugation and dialysis steps which take at least 48h are not necessary.

As it was discussed in Section 1, there are two possible pathways of fluoride reaction with MPBA; each or both could be effective in fluorescence signal quenching. This could possibly make the nanoprobe more sensitive to fluoride concentration. To see either both or just one side of the receptor accounts for decreasing the fluorescence intensity of CDs, two sets of experiments were conducted in aqueous medium. One with varied pH and the other at a fixed pH value.

First, the UV spectral change of MPBA was studied accompanied by the BrØnsted acid–base reaction with F− and OH−. With pH adjusted to 5.5, MPBA showed two absorption peaks at 255 and 287nm. When the solution pH is acidic (pH is much lower than the pka of SH group), MPBA is fully protonated and the absorption spectrum shows only one peak at 255nm (red line on Fig. 2A). At pH 5.5 thiol–thiolate equilibrium establishes and both forms coexist in solution. Therefore, we can observe two peaks; the one at 255nm corresponds to protonated (thiol) and the other at 287nm to unprotonated (thiolate form) MPBA. In the first experiment, KF was added in the concentration range of 0.0 to 0.115M. The pH of solution changed from 5.5 to 6.3. It can be seen from Fig. 2A that the peak intensity at 255nm decreased and red shifted and the one at 287nm increased.

The same experiment was done by OH− and a similar trend in spectral changes was observed (Fig. 2B). This confirms the thiol–thiolate equilibrium gradually changes toward unprotonated species. On the other hand, there is a boronic acid moiety on MPBA which can take part in Lewis acid–base reaction to afford fluoroborate anions. To investigate the spectral change of MPBA through the boronic acid head of the receptor, fluoride was added at a fixed pH of 5.5. In this case, the thiol–thiolate equilibrium remains unchanged and fluoride reacts only as a nucleophile. A different spectral trend was observed. The intensity of both peaks at 255 and 287 decreased (Fig. 2C). The former also blue shifted and the later showed a random variation on the first few injections. For better comparison, the experimental values of absorbance after successive addition of fluoride in the two cases (A and C) have been listed in Tables S1 and S2.

Next, the possibility of two mentioned reaction pathways was experimentally explored by fluorescence spectroscopy. An initial test was done in water to reveal system response to fluoride. From Fig. 3 , it is inspected that coexistence of fluoride ion and MPBA has a larger impact on signal quenching rather than MPBA alone. The same concentration of MPBA was used in the case of curves b and c (Fig. 3). In the inset of Fig. 3, fluorescence intensity change of CDs is plotted as a function of fluoride concentration before (circle) and after (triangle) addition of MPBA. Fluoride addition to the solution of CDs almost did not change the peak intensity at 340nm; while in the presence of a fixed amount of MPBA, a considerable decrease in signal intensity was observed. To facilitate the comparison of changes in these two situations, each data set was normalized. For circle line, it was normalized to the initial fluorescence intensity value of CDs and for triangle line, to CDs fluorescence signal after quenching with MPBA.

After this preliminary test, we decided to see the spectral change of CDs/MPBA mixture upon continuous addition of fluoride. When 2mM (the optimized concentration) MPBA was added to an aqueous solution of CDs, the fluorescence signal quenched and the pH of solution decreased to around 5.5. This pH was considered as the starting point. By the addition of fluoride ion, pH gradually increased and the fluorescence signal decreased accordingly (Fig. 4A). The concentration range of fluoride in this experiment was 0–27mM. In a similar experiment and same concentrations of all species, CDs/MPBA was titrated with fluoride in the range of 0-27mM with pH of solution kept constant at 5.5 (Fig. 4B). Surprisingly, no fluorescence change was observed.

In fact, if we take a closer look at UV spectral change of MPBA at a constant pH, there is a very small spectral variation in the range of 0 to around 30mM of added KF (Table S2). This means that the formation of aryltrifluoroborate anion was not appreciable to make signal quenching. The reason for this observation might be the fact that the reaction of fluoride with boron centre depends on the electronic properties of the aryl substituent [18,24,25]. In fact, at pH 5.5, both forms of MPBA coexist in solution. Although the Hammet constant value (σ) for SH group on para- position is positive (σ: 0.12 [26]), this substituent seems not to be enough electron withdrawing to capture lower concentrations of fluoride from aqueous solution and drive the formation of appreciable amount of aryltrifluoroborates. In fact, according to UV and fluorescence spectroscopy results, it can be concluded that under this condition, conversion of B(OH)2 to BF3 is almost retarded. As a result, no PET and signal quenching was observed. On the other hand, S− is highly electron donating (σ: -1.21 [26]) which prevents the Lewis acid–base pathway. Therefore, the BrØnsted acid–base reaction is prevailed and would be considered as the only reaction pathway for CDs fluorescence quenching.

The role of SH group on para- position of phenylboronic acid was assessed by comparison of the system response toward fluoride ion with respect to unsubstituted PBA. Quenching ability of both molecules was tested under the same experimental conditions. From the inset of Fig. 5 , it is well supported that MPBA contribution to signal quenching was more than that of PBA. This observation showed that MPBA interacts with CDs surface through the thiol/thiolate head. In fact, the only reaction pathway of PBA with fluoride ion is Lewis acid–base fluoridation of boronic acid moiety. From the experimental fluorescence data (Fig. 5) of both reactions (i.e. proton exchange of SH group on MPBA and fluoridation reaction of B(OH)2 on PBA) and also the Gibbs free energy (ΔG) values, it is well supported that BrØnsted acid–based reaction of MPBA is favoured over the Lewis acid–base reaction of PBA. (Please see the supporting information for ΔG values from DFT calculations).

In order to maximize the difference between fluorescence signal before and after the addition of MPBA, the effects of some experimental parameters were studied. The optimum conditions were 2mM of MPBA and 5min incubation time (Fig. S2). It is clear from Fig S2 that the experimental response time could be as fast as 30s, which is quite encouraging for this nanoprobe. The most important factor which fully affected method sensitivity was MPBA concentration. Six calibration curves with various concentrations of MPBA were constructed. The maximum value of the slopes for these curves was observed with MPBA concentration adjusted to 2mM. Therefore, this concentration was selected as the optimum.

The type of fluorescence quenching in this system was simply distinguished by the dependence on temperature. It is shown in Fig. 6 that the rise in temperature is accompanied with increase in the slope of Stern–Volmer plot. The proportional enhancement of quenching with higher temperatures proves that the observed quenching is a diffusive process, dynamic quenching [27]. As the quenching behaviour in this system cleared to be dynamic, the Stern–Volmer constant is represented by K D. From our previous work [21], the fluorescence lifetime of the as-synthesized CDs was calculated to be 6.7ns. The bimolecular quenching constant, k q =5.04×1010 M−1 s−1 was simply calculated by the values of K D (338M−1) and the fluorescence lifetime. This K q is beyond the critical value expected for diffusion-controlled bimolecular rate constant (1.0×1010 M−1 s−1) [27], which signifies an efficient quenching by the quencher.

The impact of thiolate ligands on photophysical properties of QDs has been discussed in literature [20]. In fact, thiolates have been classified as electron donor ligands with hole trapping properties [28]. A study on carrier dynamics of QDs has shown that surface bound thiolates can affect the optical properties of QDs by trapping photogenerated holes on the surface of QDs. The radiative or nonradiative recombination of electron–hole in the system causes a decrease in photoluminescence signal. [20] Since CDs and QDs are somehow similar in optical properties [29], it is proposed that thiolate ligand plays the same role as for QDs in hole trapping and subsequent signal quenching. From the quenching data discussed in the previous paragraph, it seems that CDs and the unprotonated form of MPBA contact efficiently for probable electron transfer process. In addition, photoexcited CDs are excellent as both electron donors and electron acceptors [30]. Interestingly, the photoluminescence of CDs could be quenched either by electron acceptor or electron donor molecules.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the fluorescent nanoprobe
ce:section.ce:para: The correlation of fluorescence intensity (F 0/F) at 340nm was adapted well to Stern–Volmer equation with the linear range of 0 to 26.7mM and a LOD value of 1.1×10−4 M (Fig. 7 ). This LOD value is lower than the maximum fluoride level permitted by EPA (2.0×10−4 M). The high precision of the method was indicated by the RSD values for each point of the calibration curve (RSD<1.5% (n =4)). A comparison between the analytical performance of the current system and some examples of nanoparticles based probes is brought in Table S3.

To assess the system selectivity, effects of various anions on determination of fluoride were examined. Cl−, Br−, I−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, SCN−, ClO4 − were selected as the possible interfering anions. The interference effect of these anions with equal concentration (0.8mM) to that of fluoride was negligible as depicted in Fig. 8 . Other anions like phosphate, acetate and carbonate were also tested. In fact, these three anions adversely affected the fluorescence signal which was expected because these ions could also deprotonates MPBA. Some examples of urea based receptors have been reported in literature which respond to phosphate/acetate as well [31–33]. In these examples, phosphate/acetate could also trigger the BrØnsted acid–base reaction. So, the molecular receptors in these works are not prerogative of fluoride. Also, in a report based on the FRET modulation in QDs-Ca2+ system, coexistence of some ions such as carbonate, phosphate and oxalate with fluoride caused interference in the detection system [16]. In the present work, for toothpaste analysis, phosphate ions were removed by a simple precipitation procedure [34].

To prove the applicability of this system in detecting fluoride ion in water samples, concentration of fluoride recovered from spiked river water sample was measured. Recoveries of the spiked fluoride were satisfactory (97–105.9%). There was a linear response between the fluorescence signal and fluoride concentration with a good correlation coefficient (R 2 =0.9977). The next complex real sample which was analyzed with the present method was anti-cavity toothpaste from the Crest Company with fluoride and phosphate as the only anions in its composition. Knowing phosphate interference, it was simply precipitated as aluminum phosphate and separated from the solution [34]. The amount of fluoride as the active ingredient of this mouthwash product was claimed to be 0.321%. To analyze the fluoride content of this product by the present nanoprobe, the standard addition method was used and the fluoride amount was directly derived from the regression equation. The calculated value of fluoride with this method matched well with the claimed fluoride value on product information. While the claimed value after dissolution in water was calculated to be 0.399mM, the value obtained by the present method and regression equation was found to be 0.410mM. The close agreement of these values suggests the successful applicability of this method in analysis of real samples with complex matrix. Recovery percentages of 96.1–107.7 demonstrated that this method works well with high accuracy in toothpaste as a complex matrix. The analytical data for the two real samples are summarized in Table 1 .

In summary, a CDs based fluorescent probe was designed and successfully applied to fluoride detection in toothpaste and river water with recovery values over the range of 96.1–107.7% and 97.0–105.9%, respectively. The simplicity and fast response of this nanoprobe is advantageous for analytical determinations and environmental monitoring. A few ions showed interference in this system. However, by elimination of phosphate, this nanoprobe could operate well in complex mixtures like toothpaste. Currently, we are working on other quenchers and doing more investigations on substituent effect to design a system with better sensitivity and selectivity.
UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of synthesized CDs, optimization of the experimental parameters, UV spectral data upon addition of fluoride and DFT calculations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to express their gratitude to Iran's National Elites Foundation and Shiraz University Research Council for the support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.07.096.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluoride in drinking water: a review on the status and stress effects
Fluoride: A toxic or therapeutic agent in the treatment of osteoporosis?
Neurotransmitter and receptor changes in the brains of fetuses from areas of endemic fluorosis
Recognition and sensing of fluoride anion
Nature of urea-fluoride interaction: incipient and definitive proton transfer
New polyamide cryptand for anion binding
Poly(methyl methacrylate)s with pendant calixpyrroles: polymeric extractants for halide anion salts
Synthesis of novel colorimetric probe molecules and their application in anion recognition based on strong hydrogen bond
Ratiometric sensing of fluoride anions based on a BODIPY-coumarin platform
Fluorescent probe for detection of fluoride in water and bioimaging in A549 human lung carcinoma cells
Anion capture and sensing with cationic boranes: on the synergy of Coulombic effects and onium ion-centred Lewis acidity
Fluoride ion complexation by a cationic borane in aqueous solution
Fluoride ion capture from water with a cationic borane
Zr(H2O)2EDTA modulated luminescent carbon dots as fluorescent probes for fluoride detection
Quantum dot based turn-on fluorescent probes for anion sensing
Hydrogen bond breakage by fluoride anions in a simple CdTe quantum dot/gold nanoparticle FRET system and its analytical application
Fluoride ion complexation and sensing using organoboron compounds
Selective fluorescence detection of fluoride using boronic acids
Effect of the thiol-thiolate equilibrium on the photophysical properties of aqueous CdSe/ZnS nanocrystal quantum dots
Facile approach to the synthesis of carbon nanodots and their peroxidase mimetic function in azo dyes degradation
High-performance carbon composite electrode based on an ionic liquid as a binder
Ionic liquids as precursors for highly luminescent, surface-different nitrogen-doped carbon dots used for label-free detection of Cu2+/Fe3+ and cell imaging
Substituent and solvent effects on the reactions of organoboronic acids with fluoride
Substituent effects on aryltrifluoroborate solvolysis in water: implications for Suzuki-Miyaura coupling and the design of stable (18)F-labeled aryltrifluoroborates for use in PET imaging
A survey of Hammett substituent constants and resonance and field parameters
The role of ligands in determining the exciton relaxation dynamics in semiconductor quantum dots
Nanocrystalline semiconductors: synthesis, properties, and perspectives
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Why on interaction of urea-based receptors with fluoride, beautiful colors develop
Simple bis-thiocarbono-hydrazones as sensitive, selective, colorimetric, and switch-on fluorescent chemosensors for fluoride anions
Precipitation of phosphate from solution using aluminum salt

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mojtaba Shamsipur has a PhD in analytical chemistry obtained at Michigan State University in 1979. He is currently a professor of chemistry at Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran. His research work has been mainly focused on the thermodynamic and kinetic studies of macrocyclic ligand complexes, and their analytical applications in areas such as ion-transport through liquid membranes, solid-phase extraction, ion-selective-electrodes and optical sensors. He has published over 500 scientific papers in international journals.

Afsaneh Safavi received her Ph. D. degree in Analytical Chemistry in 1980 from Birmingham University, Birmingham, UK. She is currently Professor of Analytical Chemistry with Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. Her current research interests include electrochemical and optical sensors and nanosensors, nanochemistry and environmental chemistry.

Zahra Mohammadpour received a her M.S. in Analytical Chemistry from Shiraz University in 2010. Currently she is a PhD student at Shiraz University. Her research interests include carbon based nanomaterials, FRET sensors and semiconductor quantum dots.

Amin Reza Zolghadr received his Ph. D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. He is currently Assistant professor of Physical Chemistry with Chemistry Department, Shiraz University, Shiraz, Iran. His current research interests include Statistical Mechanics, Molecular Simulation, and Surface Chemistry.
